original_abstract:
Recent advancements in model scaling have enabled large language models (LLMs) to perform a wide array of natural language processing (NLP) tasks in a zero-shot manner, requiring no additional data for task adaptation. The release of ChatGPT, in particular, has captivated the NLP community due to its remarkable ability to generate human-like responses and refine its output through interactive dialogue. However, its potential as a versatile, general-purpose task solver remains largely unexamined.

In this study, we provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of ChatGPT’s zero-shot learning capabilities across 20 popular NLP datasets spanning seven core task categories. Our findings highlight both the impressive strengths and the inherent limitations of the model. While ChatGPT excels in areas requiring complex reasoning—such as arithmetic tasks—it continues to struggle with specialized tasks like sequence tagging. Alongside our quantitative results, we offer a series of qualitative case studies to provide a deeper understanding of its performance and behavior across different linguistic challenges.
humanized_abstract:
In our recent work, we discovered that with model scaling techniques, LLMs can be applied to a large number of natural language processing (NLP) tasks in a zero-shot setting, i.e. without requiring any additional fine-tuning or calibration data. When we learned of ChatGPT, we were impressed by the fact that it is capable of producing highly human-like responses and can iteratively refine its output given feedback from the user. It is less clear, however, what the full scope of its capabilities as a general-purpose task solver are.

We present an empirical study to investigate the strengths and weaknesses of ChatGPT and examine whether its successes and failures hold across diverse sets of NLP tasks. To this end, we conduct thorough experiments using 20 widely used benchmarks for 7 mainstream NLP tasks. Our experimental results demonstrate both the extraordinary abilities and the unique drawbacks of ChatGPT. For instance, it yields stunning performance for some math-related reasoning tasks, but appears to be heavily impeded when dealing with the sequence tagging task. We also conduct a number of case studies to showcase the model’s performance and particular behaviours when being put to the test of various linguistic constructs.

We present an empirical study to investigate the strengths and weaknesses of ChatGPT and examine whether its successes and failures hold across diverse sets of NLP tasks. document.input:
Recent advancements in model scaling have enabled large language models (LLMs) to perform a wide array of natural language processing (NLP) tasks in a zero-shot manner, requiring no additional data for task adaptation. The release of ChatGPT, in particular, has captivated the NLP community due to its remarkable ability to generate human-like responses and refine its output through interactive dialogue. However, its potential as a versatile, general-purpose task solver remains largely unexamined.

In this study, we provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of ChatGPT’s zero-shot learning capabilities across 20 popular NLP datasets spanning seven core task categories. Our findings highlight both the impressive strengths and the inherent limitations of the model. While ChatGPT excels in areas requiring complex reasoning—such as arithmetic tasks—it continues to struggle with specialized tasks like sequence tagging. Alongside our quantitative results, we offer a series of qualitative case studies to provide a deeper understanding of its performance and behavior across different linguistic challenges.
